steps.title: Map the logical relationship
steps.content: The passage opens by saying Modernista architects "championed nature in their designs." The blank then begins a sentence noting that the archways and tilework of Casa Amatller "couldn't exactly grow in a forest." The word "Still" later in the passage signals that natural influences do appear. So the blank sentence is an obvious concession: yes, the architects loved nature, but of course their built elements aren't literally natural. The transition must introduce an obvious, clarifying concession — not a result, addition, or comparison.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Of course,': introduces the self-evident concession that architectural flourishes aren't real plants, bridging the nature-championing claim and the "Still" that follows. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Thus,': signals a logical result or conclusion, but the sentence is a concession, not a consequence of championing nature. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Similarly.': implies the next idea parallels the previous one, but the sentence introduces a contrasting limitation, not a parallel point. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Furthermore.': adds to the previous idea, but the sentence qualifies rather than extends the claim about nature. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
